region_of_origin: Singapore (Turkish-style, served in kebab/shawarma restaurant context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Turkish-style grilled fish. Closest defensible match is the generic grilled fish food product class (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, grilled) is the closest defensible category. No specific Turkish-style spiced grilled fish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Turkish-style grilled fish dish. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared ethnic fish dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (1 plate), representing a grilled or baked white fish fillet with Turkish-style spice marinade, typically served with a side of rice, salad, or flatbread in a kebab/shawarma restaurant context in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat; FDC ID 175168 (SR Legacy), adapted for Turkish-style spiced grilled fish preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a specific entry for 'Dragon Fish' as a Turkish-style spiced grilled fish dish sold in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 175168 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) as the primary approved anchor. Sodium is elevated to 400 mg/100g to reflect soy sauce and salt marinade components consistent with the input record. Carbohydrate (8.33 g/100g) reflects the spice coating and possible cornstarch or breadcrumb component. Fat (15.0 g/100g) reflects olive oil in the marinade. Input values from the GrabFood ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) are retained where plausible against the USDA grilled fish baseline. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain a matching Turkish-style grilled fish entry. All values are documented as estimated/adapted. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dragon Fish (Turkish-style grilled fish). GI estimated at approximately 30 based on analogous grilled/baked white fish with spice marinade from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal). Plain grilled fish has a GI of approximately 0 (negligible carbohydrate); the spice coating and carbohydrate component (8.33 g/100g) elevate the effective GI to approximately 30 (low category), consistent with mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate fish dishes. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 8.33 × 3) / 100 = 7.5 g, classified as low GL. The University of Sydney GI Database is the approved GI-specific source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and soy sauce in preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium sides such as pita bread or pickled vegetables.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 8.33 g/100g with sugar only 1.67 g/100g; high protein (11.67 g/100g) and fat (15 g/100g) slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response; estimated GI of 30 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dragon Fish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice or pita bread; monitor total meal carbohydrate load and request grilled preparation without starch coating.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat at 15 g/100g with saturated fat at 2.67 g/100g is moderate; white fish provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium preparation and grilled rather than fried cooking to preserve the cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acid profile of white fish while minimising sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (bell peppers, chilli, tomatoes) may trigger solanine-related joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerol.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with nightshade sensitivity should request substitution of bell peppers, chilli, and tomatoes; grilled preparation is preferred over fried to avoid advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation that worsens joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Fish is a Turkish-style spiced grilled or baked white fish dish sold in Singapore's kebab and shawarma restaurant segment, listed on GrabFood. The dish features a white fish fillet — likely sea bass, snapper, or similar mild-flavoured white fish — marinated in a Turkish-inspired spice blend including turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil, then grilled or baked to produce a lightly charred, aromatic exterior with a moist, flaky interior. It is typically served as a complete plate with a side of steamed rice, flatbread (pita or lavash), and a fresh salad of tomatoes, cucumber, and onion. The dish reflects the growing Turkish kebab cuisine presence in Singapore's multicultural food landscape. Nutritionally, Dragon Fish provides moderate protein (11.67 g/100g) and fat (15 g/100g) with relatively low carbohydrate (8.33 g/100g), making it a moderate-calorie dish at approximately 217 kcal/100g. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100g due to soy sauce and salt in the marinade, which is a concern for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 30), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-glycaemic accompaniments. The dish is halal-certified in the kebab restaurant context. Turmeric and ginger in the spice blend provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols (curcumin and gingerol) with evidence-based metabolic benefits. The dish is gluten-free when prepared without cornstarch coating or pita bread.
common_user_queries: Is Dragon Fish good for diabetes?

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central exact match, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a specific entry for 'Dragon Fish' as a Turkish-style spiced grilled fish dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) with sodium and carbohydrate adjusted to reflect the marinade and spice coating. The input GrabFood ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) was used as a secondary reference where plausible. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No approved source contains a specific 'Dragon Fish' Turkish-style grilled fish entry. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) — an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 — with sodium elevated to 400 mg/100g to reflect the soy sauce and salt marinade, and carbohydrate set to 8.33 g/100g to reflect spice coating and possible cornstarch component. Fat (15.0 g/100g) reflects olive oil in the marinade. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted and does not contain a matching entry. Input GrabFood ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are retained where plausible against the USDA baseline. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profiles, and moisture data for this specific Turkish-style spiced grilled fish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 3. GI ESTIMATION: Glycemic index of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) for mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate fish dishes. No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 8.33 × 3) / 100 = 7.5 g (low GL). 4. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are approximate matches to the generic grilled fish category; no specific Turkish-style spiced grilled fish codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024. SNOMED CT is set to null as no defensible code was identified. Match types are 'closest_parent'. 5. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: This dish is sold in Singapore in a Turkish kebab/shawarma restaurant context. Multilingual names (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided per Singaporean dish requirements under Project Veritas v2 Section 7. The name 'Dragon Fish' is a menu marketing name rather than a traditional Turkish culinary term. 6. DUAL DEFAULT PORTIONS: The input record contains two portions both marked is_default=true (300 g and 450 g). The 300 g portion (1 plate, fish only) is used as the canonical serving size for GL calculation. 7. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is set to 0.17 per the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from cooking oils used in grilling.
